hypothesis: Chemical foundation models pre-trained on large-scale molecular SMILES representations can generate transferable embeddings that, when combined with composition-weighted aggregation and device-level contextual variables, enable accurate prediction of battery material properties across multiple scales—from molecular quantum properties to device performance—outperforming traditional Morgan Fingerprint baselines, because the self-supervised pre-training captures generalizable physicochemical representations that encode structure-property relationships relevant to electrochemical systems even without task-specific training data.
